elements.sent-2411-002: To design Bayesian studies, criteria for the operating characteristics of posterior analyses – such as power and the type I error rate – are often assessed by estimating sampling distributions of posterior probabilities via simulation.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we propose an economical method to determine optimal sample sizes and decision criteria for such studies.
elements.sent-2411-004: Using our theoretical results that model posterior probabilities as a function of the sample size, we assess operating characteristics throughout the sample size space given simulations conducted at only two sample sizes.
elements.sent-2411-005: These theoretical results are used to construct bootstrap confidence intervals for the optimal sample sizes and decision criteria that reflect the stochastic nature of simulation-based design.
elements.sent-2411-006: We also repurpose the simulations conducted in our approach to efficiently investigate various sample sizes and decision criteria using contour plots.
elements.sent-2411-007: The broad applicability and wide impact of our methodology is illustrated using two clinical examples.
elements.sent-2411-008: Keywords: Assurance; bootstrap; experimental design; sample size determination; quantile estimation.
elements.sent-2411-009: In Bayesian methods for data-driven decision making, a scalar estimand often quantifies the impact of two choices available to decision makers.
elements.sent-2411-010: Such decisions are regularly informed by assessing a hypothesis $H_1$ based on the posterior distribution of this estimand.
elements.sent-2411-011: Decision-making methods with posterior probabilities have been proposed in various settings (see e.g., \citet{berry2010bayesian, brutti2014bayesian, stevens2022comparative ).}
elements.sent-2411-012: The observed data provide sufficient evidence to support $H_1$ if the posterior probability $Pr(H_1 ~|~ data)$ is greater than or equal to a critical value $\gamma \in [0.5, 1)$.
elements.sent-2411-013: When comparing complementary hypotheses $H_1$ and $H_0$, decision-making methods with Bayes factors \citep{jeffreys1935some, kass1995bayes, morey2011bayes can be viewed as a special case of those with posterior probabilities \citep{hagar2023fast .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: This paper therefore focuses on posterior probabilities, though the methods extend to the use of Bayes factors.
elements.sent-2411-015: It is important that decision-making methods with posterior summaries yield trustworthy conclusions.
elements.sent-2411-016: In clinical trials, regulatory agencies require that Bayesian designs are assessed with respect to frequentist operating characteristics \citep{fda2019adaptive .}
elements.sent-2411-017: These design procedures are called hybrid approaches to sample size determination since they leverage theory from Bayesian and frequentist statistics \citep{berry2010bayesian .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Decision makers in nonclinical settings may also want to control the power and type I error rate of Bayesian designs (see e.g., \citet{deng2015objective and \citet{larsen2024statistical for a discussion of operating characteristics in online A/B tests).}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Power is the probability that $Pr(H_1 ~|~ data) \ge \gamma$ according to some data generation process where $H_1$ is true.
elements.sent-2411-020: Under this data generation process, the study power should be at least $1 - \beta$.
elements.sent-2411-021: The type I error rate is the probability that $Pr(H_1 ~|~ data) \ge \gamma$ according to some data generation process where $H_1$ is false (and $H_0$ is true).
elements.sent-2411-022: Given a data generation process under $H_0$, the type I error rate for the study should not exceed $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-023: Design inputs for Bayesian studies include the hypotheses $H_1$ and $H_0$, data generation processes, prior distributions, treatment allocations, and values for $\alpha$ and $\beta$.
elements.sent-2411-024: For a particular set of design inputs, the sample size $n$ and critical value $\gamma$ determine whether criteria for the operating characteristics of the posterior analysis are satisfied.
elements.sent-2411-025: To support flexible study design, $(n, \gamma)$ combinations that control these operating characteristics can be found using intensive simulation \citep{wang2002simulation .}
elements.sent-2411-026: In general, many samples of size $n$ are simulated according to $H_1$ ($H_0$) to estimate the sampling distribution of posterior probabilities and the proportion of samples for which $Pr(H_1 ~|~ data) \ge \gamma$ estimates power (the type I error rate).
elements.sent-2411-027: This process is repeated for various sample sizes using various critical values until a suitable $(n, \gamma)$ combination is found, and this computational burden is compounded over all combinations of the design inputs that practitioners wish to investigate.
elements.sent-2411-028: Moreover, the impact of simulation variability on the recommended $(n, \gamma)$ combination is underreported since quantifying this impact often involves conducting further numerical studies \citep{wilson2021efficient .}
elements.sent-2411-029: An economical framework to determine the $(n, \gamma)$ combination that minimizes the sample size $n$ while satisfying criteria for both operating characteristics would meaningfully expedite the design of posterior analyses.
elements.sent-2411-030: Recently, several strategies have been employed to reduce the computational burden associated with controlling operating characteristics for Bayesian studies.
elements.sent-2411-031: Certain strategies are tailored to specific statistical distributions (see e.g., \citet{shi2019control for the efficient design of sequential Bernoulli trials).}
elements.sent-2411-032: Other approaches accommodate a variety of statistical models.
elements.sent-2411-033: One such general strategy leverages the parametric efficiency of beta distributions fit to the sampling distribution of posterior probabilities \citep{golchi2022estimating, golchi2023estimating .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Another general strategy prioritizes exploring segments of the sampling distribution of posterior probabilities such that $Pr(H_1 | data) \approx \gamma$ \citep{hagar2023fast .}
elements.sent-2411-035: The use of sampling distribution segments is efficient, but those methods are difficult to implement in complex design scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-036: Here, we present a remarkably simple method to design posterior analyses that does not impose parametric assumptions on the sampling distribution of $Pr(H_1 | data)$.
elements.sent-2411-037: This broadly applicable method is economical and acknowledges the variability inherent to simulation-based design.
elements.sent-2411-038: The remainder of this article is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-039: We introduce background information and notation in Section \ref{sec:intro .}
elements.sent-2411-040: In Section \ref{sec:multi , we develop theory for a proxy to the sampling distribution of posterior probabilities, and we propose a method in Section \ref{sec:multi.alg that adapts this theory to determine which $(n, \gamma)$ combination minimizes the sample size $n$ while satisfying criteria for power and the type I error rate.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: This procedure estimates the sampling distributions of posterior probabilities at only two sample sizes.
elements.sent-2411-042: Given bootstrap samples from these estimated sampling distributions, we construct confidence intervals for the optimal sample size and critical value.
elements.sent-2411-043: We illustrate the use of our design framework with examples based on semaglutide development in Section \ref{sec:multi.ex .}
elements.sent-2411-044: In Section \ref{sec:contour , we repurpose the posterior probabilities used to find the optimal $(n, \gamma)$ combination to create contour plots that facilitate the investigation of various $n$ and $\gamma$ values.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Section \ref{sec:disc concludes with a summary and discussion of extensions to this work.}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Sampling Distribution Estimation} \label{alg.int} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \setstretch{1} \Procedure{Estimate}{$f^+(\cdot)$, $g(\cdot)$, $\boldsymbol{\delta}$, $p(\boldsymbol{\eta})$, $n$, $q$, $m$, $\Psi_0$, $\Psi_1$} \For{$j$ in \{0,1\}} \For{$r$ in 1:$m$} \State Generate $\boldsymbol{\eta}^+_{j, r} \sim \Psi_j$ and $\boldsymbol{w}^{_{(n)}}_{j, r} \sim f^+(\boldsymbol{\eta}^+_{j, r})$ \State Compute estimate $\widehat{Pr}(H_1 | \boldsymbol{w}^{_{(n)}}_{j, r})$ \EndFor \EndFor \State \Return $\{\widehat{Pr}(H_1 | \boldsymbol{w}^{_{(n)}}_{1, r})\}_{r = 1}^m$ and $\{\widehat{Pr}(H_1 | \boldsymbol{w}^{_{(n)}}_{0, r})\}_{r = 1}^m$ \EndProcedure \end{algorithmic}

elements.section-2411-003: A Proxy for the Sampling Distribution
elements.sent-2411-093: Design methods to control the operating characteristics of posterior analyses require that we estimate the sampling distribution of posterior probabilities for various sample sizes $n$.
elements.sent-2411-094: We approximate such sampling distributions by generating data $\boldsymbol{w ^{_{(n) }$ using the straightforward process in Algorithm \ref{alg.int .}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: For theoretical development, we create a proxy for these sampling distributions.
elements.sent-2411-096: These proxies motivate -- but are not used in -- our design methods proposed in Section \ref{sec:multi.alg .}
elements.sent-2411-097: A detailed understanding of these proxies is not necessary to appreciate the practical benefits of our design methods that are simple and straightforward to implement, but the proxies are needed for the theory that underpins the proposed methodology.
elements.sent-2411-098: Our proxies make use of the regularity conditions listed in Appendix A of the supplement.
elements.sent-2411-099: Appendix A.1 details the four necessary assumptions to invoke the Bernstein-von Mises (BvM) theorem \citep{vaart1998bvm .}
elements.sent-2411-100: The first three assumptions are weaker than the regularity conditions for the asymptotic normality of the maximum likelihood estimator (MLE) \citep{lehmann1998theory , which are listed in Appendix A.2.}
elements.sent-2411-101: By the BvM theorem, a large-sample approximation to the posterior of $\theta~| ~\boldsymbol{w ^{_{(n) _{j, r}$ is $\mathcal{N (\hat{\theta ^{_{(n) _{j,r , \mathcal{I (\theta_{j,r )^{-1 /n)$ \citep{vaart1998bvm .}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-102: Here, $\hat{\theta ^{_{(n) _{j,r}$ is the maximum likelihood estimate of $\theta$, $\mathcal{I (\cdot)$ is the Fisher information, and $\theta_{j,r = g(\boldsymbol{\eta _{j,r )$ corresponds to $\boldsymbol{\eta _{j,r \subseteq \boldsymbol{\eta ^+_{j,r \sim \Psi_j$.}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Procedure to Determine Optimal Sample Size and Critical Value} \label{alg4} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \setstretch{1} \Procedure{Optimize}{$f^+(\cdot)$, $g(\cdot)$, $\boldsymbol{\delta}$, $p(\boldsymbol{\eta})$, $q$, $\alpha$, $\beta$, $m$, $\Psi_0$, $\Psi_1$} \State Select $n_0$ to achieve power $1-\beta$ for $\gamma = 1- \alpha$ based on the BvM theorem \For{$j$ in \{0, $1$\}} \State Estimate $\{\widehat{Pr}(H_1 | \boldsymbol{w}^{_{(n_0)}}_{j, r})\}_{r = 1}^m$ via Algorithm \ref{alg.int} and their logits $\{\hat{l}^{_{(n_0)}}_{\boldsymbol{\delta}, j, r}\}_{r = 1}^m$ \For{$r$ in $1$:$m$} \State Use the line $\hat{L}^{_{(n)}}_{\boldsymbol{\delta}, j, r}$ passing through $(n_0, \hat{l}^{_{(n_0)}}_{\boldsymbol{\delta}, j, r})$ with the slope from Theorem \ref{thm1} to get $\hat{l}^{_{(n)}}_{\boldsymbol{\delta}, j, r}$ for \hspace*{42pt} other sample sizes \EndFor \EndFor \State Use binary search to find $n_1$, the smallest $n$ such that $\xi$($\lfloor m \beta \rfloor$, $\{\hat{l}^{_{(n)}}_{\boldsymbol{\delta}, 1,r}\}_{r=1}^{m}$) $\ge$ $\xi$($\lceil m(1 - \alpha) \rceil$, $\{\hat{l}^{_{(n)}}_{\boldsymbol{\delta}, 0,r}\}_{r = 1}^{m}$) \For{$j$ in \{0, $1$\}} \State Estimate $\{\widehat{Pr}(H_1 | \boldsymbol{w}^{_{(n_1)}}_{j, r})\}_{r = 1}^m$ via Algorithm \ref{alg.int} and their logits $\{\hat{l}^{_{(n_1)}}_{\boldsymbol{\delta}, j, r}\}_{r = 1}^m$ \For{$r$ in $1$:$m$} \State Use the line $\hat{L}^{_{(n)}}_{\boldsymbol{\delta}, j, r}$ passing through $(n_0, \xi(r, \{\hat{l}^{_{(n_0)}}_{\boldsymbol{\delta}, j, r}\}_{r=1}^{m}))$ and $(n_1, \xi(r, \{\hat{l}^{_{(n_1)}}_{\boldsymbol{\delta}, j, r}\}_{r=1}^{m}))$ to get $\hat{l}^{_{(n)}}_{\boldsymbol{\delta}, j, r}$ \hspace*{42pt} for other sample sizes \EndFor \EndFor \State Use binary search to find $n_2$, the smallest $n$ such that $\xi$($\lfloor m \beta \rfloor$, $\{\hat{l}^{_{(n)}}_{\boldsymbol{\delta}, 1,r}\}_{r=1}^{m}$) $\ge$ $\xi$($\lceil m(1 - \alpha) \rceil$, $\{\hat{l}^{_{(n)}}_{\boldsymbol{\delta}, 0,r}\}_{r = 1}^{m}$) \State \Return $n_2$ as recommended $n$ and $\xi$($\lceil m(1 - \alpha) \rceil$, $\{\hat{p}^{_{(n_2)}}_{\boldsymbol{\delta}, 0, r}\}_{r = 1}^{m}$) as $\gamma$ \EndProcedure \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-308: In this paper, we developed an economical framework to design posterior analyses using operating characteristics – namely power and the type I error rate -- that determines optimal sample sizes and decision criteria.
elements.sent-2411-309: The efficiency of this framework stems from considering a proxy for the sampling distribution of posterior probabilities based on large-sample theory to justify estimating the true sampling distributions at only two sample sizes.
elements.sent-2411-310: This approach substantially reduces the number of simulation repetitions required to design posterior analyses, making them much more attractive and accessible to practitioners who want to control type I and II error.
elements.sent-2411-311: The posterior probabilities used to determine the optimal sample size and decision criteria can also be repurposed to (i) construct bootstrap confidence intervals that acknowledge simulation variability and (ii) helpfully investigate various sample sizes and decision criteria using contour plots.
elements.sent-2411-312: Our proposed methods are broadly applicable to various two-group comparisons, including those that are more complex than our illustrative examples.
elements.sent-2411-313: Nevertheless, our methods could be extended in many aspects to accommodate more complex designs.
elements.sent-2411-314: For instance, future work could consider economical design methods that account for sequential analyses allowing for early termination or the multiple comparisons problem more generally.
elements.sent-2411-315: This extension would be nontrivial because we would need to properly account for the level of dependence in the joint distribution of the posterior probabilities across all potential analyses and estimands.
elements.sent-2411-316: We could also extend our methods to accommodate Bayesian hierarchical models for dependent data; the standard BvM theorem does not apply in those settings.
elements.sent-2411-317: Furthermore, practitioners may want to consider the operating characteristics of a posterior analysis for various data generation processes $\Psi_1$ and $\Psi_0$.
elements.sent-2411-318: While we already efficiently explore the sample size space, it would be of interest to derive analogs to Theorem 1 that enable efficient consideration of the $\Psi_1$-space and $\Psi_0$-space.
elements.sent-2411-319: These materials include a detailed description of the conditions for Theorem \ref{thm1 along with its proof and additional simulation results.}
elements.sent-2411-320: The code to conduct the numerical studies in the paper is available online: \url{https://github.com/lmhagar/EconDesignPosterior .}
